item.title: Recovery centre opens in Suva
item.description: FIJI’S first dedicated alcohol and drugs recovery centre was opened in Suva last week, marking a significant step in the country’s response to substance abuse. Officially opened by Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka, the Salvation Army Alcohol and Drugs Recovery Centre was established in partnership with the Fijian Government and with funding support from New Zealand’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs and
item.content:encoded:
FIJI’S first dedicated alcohol and drugs recovery centre was opened in Suva last week, marking a significant step in the country’s response to substance abuse.

Officially opened by Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka, the Salvation Army Alcohol and Drugs Recovery Centre was established in partnership with the Fijian Government and with funding support from New Zealand’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade.

The facility will operate as a day program with a rolling weekly intake, offering participants a structured recovery program while allowing them to remain connected to their families and communities.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Yavusa members celebrate occasion
item.description: Thirty years after last hosting the Bose ni Vanua ni Yasana o Lau, the yavusa Muairewa of Qalikarua Village, on Matuku Island came together on Saturday afternoon for a traditional vakacirisalusalu at Draiba Primary School in Suva to celebrate the successful event. The thanksgiving event brought together men, women and children for an afternoon of fellowship, entertainment and cultural performances
item.content:encoded:
Thirty years after last hosting the Bose ni Vanua ni Yasana o Lau, the yavusa Muairewa of Qalikarua Village, on Matuku Island came together on Saturday afternoon for a traditional vakacirisalusalu at Draiba Primary School in Suva to celebrate the successful event.

The thanksgiving event brought together men, women and children for an afternoon of fellowship, entertainment and cultural performances, including a display by the Police DB2 band. The celebration marked the end of months of preparation by the Matuku community, whose efforts helped deliver a memorable Bose ni Vanua ni Yasana o Lau.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FSC says sugar supply improving amid temporary shortage
item.description: The Fiji Sugar Corporation says the temporary shortage of sugar in some supermarkets and retail outlets is steadily improving, with all three mills now operating and production increasing. In a statement, FSC management acknowledged the inconvenience caused to consumers, saying efforts were underway to restore normal supplies nationwide.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Sugar Corporation says the temporary shortage of sugar in some supermarkets and retail outlets is steadily improving, with all three mills now operating and production increasing.

In a statement, FSC management acknowledged the inconvenience caused to consumers, saying efforts were underway to restore normal supplies nationwide.

“The temporary shortage resulted from an unforeseen delay in the commencement of the 2026 crushing season, which placed pressure on domestic sugar stocks and supply,” FSC said.

The corporation introduced a temporary rationing system to ensure the available stock was distributed fairly among customers.

“This measure was implemented to support the equitable distribution of available stocks and minimise the risk of complete stockouts in the market,” it said.

FSC said the Lautoka Mill began crushing on July 14, while sugar-bagging operations had resumed and fresh stocks were being progressively dispatched through the local distribution network.

It said consumers should see supplies gradually returning to supermarket shelves as retailers replenish their inventories over the coming weeks.

FSC said it had made significant progress in clearing outstanding customer orders.

“The current backlog for retail-pack sugar stands at approximately 65 tonnes, and these orders are expected to be cleared by the end of this week,” management said.

All outstanding orders for 25-kilogram bags from June had been fulfilled, while deliveries were being made against current-month orders.

To support local production during the transition, FSC has secured imported sugar.

Initial shipments from New Zealand have arrived and are being distributed, while consignments from Thailand are expected throughout August and subsequent imports will continue as needed.

“We appreciate the patience and understanding shown by consumers, retailers and our stakeholders during this period,” FSC said.

The corporation expects sugar availability across retail outlets to continue improving in the coming weeks.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: State drops attempted murder charges against two men in Constable Tikoduadua case
item.description: The State has discontinued proceedings against two of the three men charged with the attempted murder of Police Constable Senoveva Tikoduadua. Senior State lawyer Bhavna Kantharia filed a nolle prosequi in the High Court before Justice Dane Tuiqereqere, resulting in the discharge of Dominiko Tabau and Jone Rakatia.
item.content:encoded:
The State has discontinued proceedings against two of the three men charged with the attempted murder of Police Constable Senoveva Tikoduadua.

Senior State lawyer Bhavna Kantharia filed a nolle prosequi in the High Court before Justice Dane Tuiqereqere, resulting in the discharge of Dominiko Tabau and Jone Rakatia.

The pair were initially charged alongside Saimoni Rokotunidau, whose case will continue.

Mr Rakatia was represented by lawyer Yogen Kumar of Jitendra Reddy Lawyers, while Mr Tabau was represented by Gavin O’Driscoll.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fijian among three escaped prisoners sought in Tonga
item.description: Tonga Police are appealing for public assistance to locate three escaped prisoners, including one man identified as the holder of a Fijian passport. In a public alert, police named the escapee Adam Zabid Hussein, who is identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.
item.content:encoded:
Tonga Police are appealing for public assistance to locate three escaped prisoners, including one man identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.

In a public alert, police named the escapee Adam Zabid Hussein, who is identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.

Authorities have urged members of the public not to approach the escapees if they are seen, warning that they should instead immediately contact emergency number 922 or the nearest police station with any information about their whereabouts.

Tonga Police also reminded the public that it is a criminal offence to knowingly assist, harbour, conceal or provide any form of aid to escaped prisoners.

The force thanked the public for its continued cooperation, vigilance and support as efforts continue to locate the three men.

Police said anyone with information that may assist the investigation is urged to contact Tonga Police immediately.

No further details have been released on how or when the prisoners escaped, and investigations are continuing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Rain to spread across Fiji from later today
item.description: Rain is expected to spread across Fiji from the west later today, with forecasters warning of isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms across most of the country by Wednesday. The National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi states a trough with associated cloud and rain, currently lying west of Fiji, is gradually moving eastwards and is expected to affect the group later today.
item.content:encoded:
Rain is expected to spread across Fiji from the west later today, with forecasters warning of isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms across most of the country by Wednesday.

The National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi states a trough with associated cloud and rain, currently lying west of Fiji, is gradually moving eastwards and is expected to affect the group later today.

Before the rain arrives, cloudy periods and some showers are forecast for Serua-Namosi, Navua-Suva-Nausori, Tailevu-Naitasiri-Ra, southern Bua, Cakaudrove, Udu Point, Taveuni and nearby islands, the Lau and Lomaiviti groups, Kadavu and nearby islands, while the rest of the country is expected to remain mainly fine.

The weather office says conditions will be cool overnight, with moderate easterly winds, fresh at times, and moderate seas.

The outlook for Wednesday is for occasional rain with isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms over most parts of Fiji as the trough moves across the country.

Authorities are advising the public to monitor the latest weather updates, particularly those in flood-prone areas, as heavy rain could lead to localised flooding.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Electoral reform proposal ‘mathematically unworkable’, report warns
item.description: A technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s proposed electoral system has concluded that the model is “not a workable proportional electoral system”, warning it contains fundamental mathematical flaws that could undermine election results. In the 13-page assessment prepared by Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal, the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency Sys
item.content:encoded:
A technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s proposed electoral system has concluded that the model is “not a workable proportional electoral system”, warning it contains fundamental mathematical flaws that could undermine election results.

In the 13-page assessment prepared by Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal, the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency System (POLCS) is described as “mathematically incomplete, internally contradictory and incapable of reliably delivering its central promise of national proportionality.”

The report argues the proposal’s central formula—calculating list seats by subtracting constituency seats from a party’s overall entitlement—fails whenever a party wins more constituency seats than its share of the national vote justifies.

Using a hypothetical example, the assessment states that if a party won all 50 constituency seats with just 51 per cent of the national vote, it would be entitled to only about 36 seats under proportional representation.

The formula would therefore require the party to receive minus 14 MPs, something the report says is mathematically impossible.

According to the assessment, only three outcomes would then be possible: remove elected constituency MPs, abandon proportional representation, or significantly increase the size of Parliament through “overhang” and levelling seats.

It says none of those solutions is explained in the proposal.

The report also criticises the proposed ratio of 50 constituency seats and only 21 compensatory list seats, saying it gives far too much weight to first-past-the-post elections before proportionality is considered.

It argues that New Zealand’s MMP system uses a much safer electorate-to-list ratio, whereas Fiji’s proposed 70:30 split creates a much greater risk of disproportional election outcomes.

The assessment concludes that unless a detailed overhang mechanism exists but has not been published, the proposal cannot guarantee proportional representation despite claims that it would.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PCC warns against militarising Pacific’s ‘Ocean of Peace’ vision
item.description: The General Secretary of the Pacific Conference of Churches (PCC), Reverend James Bhagwan, has urged Pacific leaders to ensure the region’s “Ocean of Peace” vision is not overtaken by military and security interests, warning that peace must remain rooted in justice, dialogue and the wellbeing of Pacific peoples. In a seven-page pastoral open letter titled “Let Peace Govern Security”, Rev. Bhagwan
item.content:encoded:
The General Secretary of the Pacific Conference of Churches (PCC), Reverend James Bhagwan, has urged Pacific leaders to ensure the region’s “Ocean of Peace” vision is not overtaken by military and security interests, warning that peace must remain rooted in justice, dialogue and the wellbeing of Pacific peoples.

In a seven-page pastoral open letter titled “Let Peace Govern Security”, Rev. Bhagwan reflects on the biblical passage of Micah 4:1-4 and calls on Pacific governments, churches and international partners to preserve the original intent of the Ocean of Peace initiative.

Writing from Majuro in the Marshall Islands, Rev. Bhagwan says Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka’s Ocean of Peace vision was initially welcomed because it offered the Pacific a way to promote peace through relationships, dignity, dialogue and care for creation rather than geopolitical competition.

However, he expresses concern that the concept is increasingly being linked to defence and security arrangements.

Referring to the recently signed Veitacini Treaty between Fiji and Australia, Rev. Bhagwan notes that while the agreement affirms dialogue and respect for sovereignty, it also includes provisions on deterrence, collective defence and responses to armed attacks.

He points to growing public debate over whether the Ocean of Peace vision has been “hijacked” and cites concerns raised by regional civil society leaders who fear the concept is being co-opted by external military interests.

Rev. Bhagwan argues that the Pacific’s understanding of peace must remain broader than national security.

He says Pacific churches and communities have long defined peace as the wellbeing of people, land and ocean, grounded in reconciliation, justice, neighbourliness and respect for Indigenous knowledge.

The letter also recalls the legacy of nuclear testing in the Marshall Islands, ongoing climate change, violence against women, deep-sea mining and economic inequality as some of the region’s greatest security challenges.

Rev. Bhagwan calls for the proposed Ocean of Peace Centre to focus on conflict prevention, mediation, trauma healing, social cohesion and civilian-led peacebuilding rather than military objectives.

He also urges leaders to ensure women, young people, persons with disabilities, Indigenous communities, churches and civil society share decision-making authority from the outset.

Concluding the letter, Rev. Bhagwan says security should always be guided by peace rather than the reverse.

“May peace give security its direction. May justice shape every agreement,” he writes, calling on Pacific leaders to choose dialogue over confrontation and ensure the Ocean of Peace remains under the shared guardianship of Pacific peoples.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
